summary: This 2003 workshop report explores how to scale XP and Agile methods beyond small teams, featuring perspectives from Ken Schwaber, Philippe Kruchten, and Martin Fowler. The key tension throughout is whether scaling Agile is even the right question to ask. Practitioners shared techniques like seeding new teams with architecture founders and coordinating sync points between autonomous teams. However, Martin Fowler's keynote challenged the premise entirely, arguing that many teams are unnecessarily large and should scale down rather than scale up their methodology. The conference highlighted that communication-centric practices pose the greatest scaling challenges, while coding mechanics like TDD and pair programming can scale more readily.
key_points: Scaling can be achieved by proving architecture early with a core team, then seeding new teams with original architects as founders
Communication-centric XP practices (standups, iteration planning) are harder to scale than coding practices (TDD, refactoring, pairing)
Ken Schwaber observed that average performers often rise to excellence in supportive, self-organizing team environments
Most literature targets developers who already know how to code - broader adoption requires 'cookbooks for people who don't know how to cook'
When asked if their projects could maintain productivity after removing the weakest half of the team, most attendees raised their hands
money_quotes: Business logic is an oxymoron. There is nothing logical about most aspects of business.
Today we have the cook books for people who know how to cook. What we do not have today are cook books for people who do not know how to cook.
The greatest challenge we see with scaling XP, is the same challenge we see every large project faces - how to effectively communicate and coordinate large numbers of people.
People who have been categorized as average, or even poor, often rise to excellence given a supportive, creative, team oriented environment.

Martin asked the audience to raise their hand if they had ever been on a project that could have kept about the same level of productivity after removing the weakest half of the team. The majority of the room raised their hands.
Instead of first looking at ways to scale XP and Agile, Martin recommended teams first look for ways of scaling down projects that are unnecessarily large.
key_insight: Before asking how to scale Agile methods to large teams, first ask whether your team needs to be that large in the first place.
